having-after-grouping.label: HAVING After Grouping
having-after-grouping.summary: Keep only completed groups whose aggregate results meet a meaningful threshold.

single-choice-1.title: HAVING Timing
single-choice-1.hint: HAVING can evaluate COUNT, SUM, and other aggregate results.

help.concept: Groups must exist before HAVING can test them.

single-choice-2.title: Correct Aggregate Threshold
single-choice-2.prompt: Which clause correctly keeps groups with at least four rows?
single-choice-2.hint: The condition depends on a group count.

help.hint_1: WHERE cannot use a group result that has not been calculated.

multi-choice-1.title: WHERE and HAVING Pairings
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which clauses are placed correctly for row or group filtering? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Separate row attributes from aggregate outcomes.

help.concept: Status can be tested on one row.
help.hint_1: SUM and AVG require group calculation.
help.hint_2: COUNT cannot be evaluated in WHERE.

multi-choice-2.title: Good Threshold Design
multi-choice-2.prompt: Which practices improve a HAVING exercise? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-2.hint: A useful threshold should produce interpretable results and match the metric.

help.concept: Unexpected emptiness can make correct SQL look broken.
help.hint_1: The report question should explain why the threshold matters.

options.a: Check the threshold against seeded data.
options.c: Prefer an empty result even when emptiness is not the lesson.
options.d: Use units that match the aggregate metric.

drag-reorder-1.title: Logical Group Filtering
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the logical stages that lead to HAVING filtering.
drag-reorder-1.hint: HAVING cannot run until aggregate values exist.

help.hint_2: The final stage keeps qualifying groups.

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Complete the Group Filter
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Fill the clause that keeps categories with four or more completed orders.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: The condition tests an aggregate count.

help.concept: Use the group-filtering clause after GROUP BY.

try_having_after_grouping_sketch0.title: Keep Categories with Several Completed Orders
try_having_after_grouping_sketch0.prompt: Return `category` and completed-order count as `completed_orders`. Keep categories with at least four completed orders and sort highest count first, then category.
try_having_after_grouping_sketch0.hint: The status filter belongs in WHERE; the count threshold belongs in HAVING.

help.concept: HAVING decides which aggregate group rows remain after COUNT is calculated.
help.hint_1: Use the aggregate expression in the threshold.
help.hint_2: Add a stable category tie-breaker after the count sort.

try_having_after_grouping_sketch0.solutionCode:
SELECT
    category,
    COUNT(*) AS completed_orders
FROM sales_reporting
WHERE order_status = 'Completed'
GROUP BY category
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 4
ORDER BY completed_orders DESC, category ASC;

try_having_after_grouping_sketch1.title: Find High-Value Categories
try_having_after_grouping_sketch1.prompt: Return each `category` and gross sales rounded to two decimals as `gross_sales`. Keep categories whose gross sales exceed 550, then sort highest first.
try_having_after_grouping_sketch1.hint: Repeat the aggregate expression in HAVING and use the alias for display sorting.

help.concept: A group threshold must be compared with a completed aggregate value.
help.hint_1: Do not place the SUM condition in WHERE.
help.hint_2: Keep the threshold at 550 so the seeded result remains non-empty.

try_having_after_grouping_sketch1.solutionCode:
SELECT
    category,
    ROUND(SUM(quantity * unit_price), 2) AS gross_sales
FROM sales_reporting
GROUP BY category
HAVING SUM(quantity * unit_price) > 550
ORDER BY gross_sales DESC, category ASC;

sketch0.title: Filter the Completed Summary Rows
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
`HAVING` filters result groups after aggregate values have been calculated. It is the grouped-report counterpart to WHERE.

```sql
SELECT
    category,
    COUNT(*) AS completed_orders
FROM sales_reporting
WHERE order_status = 'Completed'
GROUP BY category
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 4;
```

WHERE first limits the source rows to completed orders. GROUP BY creates one summary per category. HAVING then keeps only categories whose completed-order count reaches the threshold.

Use HAVING when the condition depends on a count, sum, average, or another aggregate result.

sketch1.title: Use a Calibrated Monetary Threshold
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
A HAVING rule should be meaningful for the current data and the decision being made.

```sql
SELECT
    region,
    ROUND(SUM(quantity * unit_price), 2) AS gross_sales
FROM sales_reporting
GROUP BY region
HAVING SUM(quantity * unit_price) > 450;
```

This keeps regional groups whose gross sales exceed $450. The threshold is applied to each completed aggregate, not to individual order values.

Calibrating the threshold matters: an impossible rule produces an empty report even when the query is correct.
